title: Family support to be considered before NDIS funds granted
full_text:
BUDGET 2026 People seeking access to the NDIS will need to “undertake all appropriate treatments” for a condition before it’s considered permanent, as part of landmark legislation that will put financial sustainability at the heart of funding decisions and consider someone’s family supports and access to other services before giving them an NDIS plan.
The bill follows revelations in Tuesday’s budget that Labor will seek to reduce NDIS annual growth to as low as 0.2 per cent in the next two years, before growth normalises at 5 per cent in the long term, and generate an estimated $185bn through its changes over the decade.
NDIS and Health Minister Mark Butler on Thursday morning introduced the legislation, known as the “securing the NDIS for future generations bill”, which he said was critical for the survival of the scheme.
“All plans need to have an end date and unspent funds will not be carried over to new plans,” he told parliament as he tabled the bill.
“The interpretation of what is reasonable and necessary to fund has broadened over time.” In order to tighten the scope of what is funded, the bill proposes to have scheme sustainability and value for money considered in funding decisions, along with what family supports are available to a person.
Mr Butler said the changes would allow for the agency to reduce care where it may not be needed, for example by reducing the number of average hours of therapy a year from 72 to 68.
“When we were looking through different ways to manage the cost growth in the NDIS, (we) were keen to ensure that we could quarantine those more critical areas of support like activities of daily living, travel and transport to medical appointments, accommodation supports and such,” he told reporters in Canberra, following the introduction of the bill.
Besides putting end dates on plans, preventing unused funding from being carried over by participants and scrutinising the total care needed per person, the legislation will also make the disability agency consider more closely what other services an NDIS participant could be eligible for.
“Currently, NDIS access decision makers do not need to consider whether a person is eligible to receive supports through another service system, such as state and territory workers’ compensation or motor accident schemes,” the legislation tabled on Thursday states.
“Under this (bill), primary legislative amendments would clarify the requirement for the NDIA to consider at access whether a person is eligible for similar supports through other alternative services systems.” Despite concerns following the Robodebt scandal, the bill will allow for the use of automated decision making, which Mr Butler argued would focus on administration and freeing up agency staff for more complex tasks.
“Being able to automate some of these basic administrative functions frees up our highly qualified public servants, frankly, to do more work with actual interaction with human beings where that interaction is particularly important,” he said.
The legislation was met with a mixed response, with some concerned about potential side effects. Former NDIA chief economist David Cullen said the definitions and thresholds could fundamentally undermine the core tenants of the scheme. “In the new world, a support can be determined by the agency to be reasonable and necessary and yet not be funded. That makes no sense,” he said.
summary: New legislation will require people to complete all appropriate treatments before accessing NDIS funds, considering family support and other services. The changes aim to reduce NDIS growth to 0.2% over two years and save $185 billion over the next decade.
author: SARAH ISON, SENIOR POLITICAL REPORT
heading: General Insurance
key_points: People must undertake all treatments before NDIS is granted.
NDIS growth will be reduced to 0.2% for two years.
Legislation introduced by NDIS and Health Minister Mark Butler.
Unused funds will not carry over to new plans.
Agency will consider other service eligibility before granting NDIS access.
Automated decision-making will be used for administrative tasks.
ticker_line: New NDIS rules focus on treatment and family support
